title.raw: 卷九十三 佞幸傳 Volume 93: Flatterers

sentences.zh: 卷九十三 佞幸傳第六十三

translations.literal: Volume ninety-three: Biography of the Flatterers, the sixty-third.
translations.idiomatic: Volume 93: "The Flatterers," the sixty-third biography.

sentences.zh: 漢興，佞幸寵臣，高祖時則有籍孺，孝惠有閎孺。

translations.literal: When the Han arose, among the flattering favorites who won imperial favor, in Gaozu's time there was Ji Ru, and in Xiaohui's time there was Hong Ru.
translations.idiomatic: After the Han rose, the court had its share of favorites: under Gaozu there was Ji Ru; under Emperor Hui, Hong Ru.

sentences.zh: 此兩人非有材能，但以婉媚貴幸，與上臥起，公卿皆因關說。

translations.literal: These two men had no talent or ability; they relied solely on ingratiating charm to become noble and favored, slept and rose with the sovereign, and the ministers all used them to convey pleas.
translations.idiomatic: Neither had any real ability; they rose by soft flattery alone, sharing the emperor's bedchamber, and high officials routinely used them as go-betweens to reach the throne.

sentences.zh: 故孝惠時，郎侍中皆冠鵔鸃，貝帶，傅脂粉，化閎、籍之屬也。

translations.literal: Therefore in Xiaohui's time the gentlemen of the palace and attendants-in-chief all wore zījī caps, shell belts, and applied face powder—transformed into the likes of Hong and Ji.
translations.idiomatic: So under Emperor Hui, Gentlemen of the Palace and attendants-in-chief took to zījī caps, shell-inlaid belts, and rouge—aping the style of Hong Ru and Ji Ru.

sentences.zh: 兩人徙家安陵。

translations.literal: The two men moved their households to Anling.
translations.idiomatic: Both families were resettled at Anling.

sentences.zh: 其後寵臣，孝文時士人則鄧通，宦者則趙談、北宮伯子；

translations.literal: Among the later favorites: in Xiaowen's time the commoner was Deng Tong, the eunuchs were Zhao Tan and Beigong Bozi;
translations.idiomatic: Later reigns had their own pets: under Emperor Wen, the outsider was Deng Tong; the eunuchs were Zhao Tan and Beigong Bozi;

sentences.zh: 孝武時士人則韓嫣，宦者則李延年；

translations.literal: in Xiaowu's time the commoner was Han Yan, the eunuch was Li Yannian;
translations.idiomatic: under Emperor Wu, the courtier was Han Yan and the eunuch Li Yannian;

sentences.zh: 孝元時宦者則弘恭、石顯；

translations.literal: in Xiaoyuan's time the eunuchs were Hong Gong and Shi Xian;
translations.idiomatic: under Emperor Yuan, the eunuchs Hong Gong and Shi Xian;

sentences.zh: 孝成時士人則張放、淳于長；

translations.literal: in Xiaocheng's time the commoners were Zhang Fang and Chunyu Chang;
translations.idiomatic: under Emperor Cheng, Zhang Fang and Chunyu Chang;

sentences.zh: 孝哀時則有董賢。

translations.literal: in Xia'ai's time there was Dong Xian.
translations.idiomatic: and under Emperor Ai, Dong Xian.

sentences.zh: 孝景、昭、宣時皆無寵臣。

translations.literal: In Xiaojing's, Zhao's, and Xuandi's times there were none who were favorites in this sense.
translations.idiomatic: Emperors Jing, Zhao, and Xuan had no true "favorites" of this kind.

sentences.zh: 景帝唯有郎中令周仁。

translations.literal: Emperor Jing had only Palace Commandant Zhou Ren.
translations.idiomatic: Jing's circle held only Zhou Ren, the palace commandant.

sentences.zh: 昭帝時，駙馬都尉秺侯金賞嗣父車騎將軍日磾爵為侯，二人之寵取過庸，不篤。

translations.literal: In Zhaodi's time, Commandant of the Imperial Son-in-Law and Marquis of Du Jin Shang inherited his father's chariot-and-cavalry general Jin Midi's marquisate; the favor shown the two of them exceeded the ordinary, but it was not deep.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Zhao, Jin Shang, marquis of Du and commandant of the imperial son-in-law, inherited his father Jin Midi's chariot-and-cavalry rank and marquisate; both father and son enjoyed modest favor—nothing excessive.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，侍中中郎將張彭祖少與帝微時同席研書，及帝即尊位，彭祖以舊恩封陽都侯，出常參乘，號為愛幸。

translations.literal: In Xuandi's time, Palace Attendant and General of the Household Zhang Pengzu had studied at the same mat with the emperor in humble days; when the emperor took the throne, Pengzu was enfeoffed marquis of Yangdu for old kindness, regularly rode as guard in the secondary chariot, and was called beloved and favored.
translations.idiomatic: Xuandi's childhood study-mate Zhang Pengzu, later a palace attendant and general of the household, was made marquis of Yangdu for past loyalty and often rode beside him as escort—people spoke of him as a favorite.

sentences.zh: 其人謹敕，無所虧損，為其小妻所毒薨，國除。

translations.literal: The man was cautious and restrained and did nothing improper; he died poisoned by a junior wife, and the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: He was careful and irreproachable until a concubine poisoned him; his fief was struck from the rolls.

sentences.zh: 鄧通，蜀郡南安人也，以濯船為黃頭郎。

translations.literal: Deng Tong was a man of Nan'an in Shu commandery; he served as a Yellow-Turban Gentleman who bailed boats.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong of Nan'an in Shu had been a Yellow-Turban boatman in the imperial service.

sentences.zh: 文帝嘗夢欲上天，不能，有一黃頭郎推上天，顧見其衣尻帶後穿。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen once dreamed he wished to ascend to heaven but could not; there was a Yellow-Turban Gentleman who pushed him up, and when he looked back he saw the seam at the back of the man's trousers was worn through.
translations.idiomatic: Wen once dreamed he could not rise to heaven until a Yellow-Turban attendant shoved him upward; glancing back, he noticed the seat of the man's trousers was split.

sentences.zh: 覺而之漸台，以夢中陰目求推者郎，見鄧通，其衣後穿，夢中所見也。

translations.literal: When he awoke he went to the Jian Terrace and, with the dream still in mind, sought the gentleman who had pushed him; he saw Deng Tong, whose clothes were worn through at the back as in the dream.
translations.idiomatic: He went to the Jian Terrace and quietly searched for that attendant—and found Deng Tong, whose trousers were torn exactly where the dream had shown.

sentences.zh: 召問其名姓，姓鄧，名通。

translations.literal: He summoned him and asked his surname and given name: the surname was Deng, the given name Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Asked his name, the man said he was Deng Tong.

sentences.zh: 鄧猶登也，文帝甚說，尊幸之，日日異。

translations.literal: Deng is like deng ("to ascend"); the emperor was greatly pleased, honored and favored him, and day by day it grew more extraordinary.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor took "Deng" for "ascend" and was delighted; Deng Tong's favor climbed higher every day.

sentences.zh: 通亦願謹，不好外交，雖賜洗沐，不欲出。

translations.literal: Tong was also willing and careful, did not like dealings with outsiders, and even when granted leave to bathe did not wish to go out.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong was obliging and discreet, avoided outside contacts, and even on bath-leave preferred not to leave the palace.

sentences.zh: 於是文帝賞賜通巨萬以十數，官至上大夫。

translations.literal: Thereupon Emperor Wen rewarded Deng Tong with tens of counts of a hundred thousand cash, and his office rose to senior grandee.
translations.idiomatic: Wen showered him with fortunes counted in the hundreds of thousands and promoted him to senior grandee.

sentences.zh: 文帝時間如通家遊戲，然通無他技能，不能有所薦達，獨自謹身以媚上而已。

translations.literal: From time to time Emperor Wen visited Tong's house for amusement; yet Tong had no other skills, could recommend no one for advancement, and only kept himself careful to flatter the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Wen sometimes dropped in on Deng Tong's home for sport, but Deng had no talent for recommending men—only a knack for keeping himself in the emperor's good graces.

sentences.zh: 上使善相人者相通，曰：「當貧餓死。

translations.literal: The sovereign had a skilled physiognomist read Tong and he said: "He will die poor and starving.
translations.idiomatic: Wen had a face-reader study Deng Tong, who said, "This man will starve in poverty.

sentences.zh: 」上曰：「能富通者在我，何說貧？

translations.literal: " The sovereign said: "Who can enrich Tong is myself—what talk of poverty?"
translations.idiomatic: " "Who makes Deng Tong rich is me," replied the emperor. "How could he end poor?"

sentences.zh: 」於是賜通蜀嚴道銅山，得自鑄錢。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he granted Tong the copper mountains at Yandao in Shu, so that he could mint coin himself.
translations.idiomatic: " So he gave Deng Tong the Yandao copper hills in Shu and let him cast his own cash.

sentences.zh: 鄧氏錢布天下，其富如此。

translations.literal: The coin of the Deng clan spread through the realm; such was his wealth.
translations.idiomatic: Deng Tong's money circulated across the empire—such was his wealth.

sentences.zh: 文帝嘗病癰，鄧通常為上嗽吮之。

translations.literal: Emperor Wen once suffered from a boil, and Deng Tong often sucked the infection for the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: When Wen developed an abscess, Deng Tong habitually drew the pus with his mouth.

sentences.zh: 上不樂，從容問曰：「天下誰最愛我者乎？

translations.literal: The sovereign was unhappy and asked at ease: "Who in the realm loves me most?"
translations.idiomatic: Unhappy, Wen asked casually, "Who in all the world loves me most?"

sentences.zh: 」通曰：「宜莫若太子。

translations.literal: " Tong said: "None should compare to the heir apparent."
translations.idiomatic: " "Surely no one loves you more than the crown prince," Deng Tong said.

sentences.zh: 」太子入問疾，上使太子齰癰。

translations.literal: " When the heir apparent came in to inquire after his illness, the sovereign had the heir apparent suck the boil.
translations.idiomatic: " When the crown prince visited the sickbed, Wen told him to suck the sore.

sentences.zh: 太子齰癰而色難之。

translations.literal: The heir apparent sucked the boil but his expression showed difficulty.
translations.idiomatic: The prince did it, but his face showed revulsion.

sentences.zh: 已而聞通嘗為上齰之，太子慚，由是心恨通。

translations.literal: Afterward, when he heard that Tong had often sucked it for the sovereign, the heir apparent was ashamed and from this inwardly hated Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that Deng Tong had done the same for his father, the prince was mortified and nursed a grudge against him.

sentences.zh: 及文帝崩，景帝立，鄧通免，家居。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wen died and Emperor Jing took the throne, Deng Tong was dismissed and lived at home.
translations.idiomatic: When Wen died and Jing succeeded, Deng Tong was stripped of office and sent home.

sentences.zh: 居無何，人有告通盜出徼外鑄錢，下吏驗問，頗有，遂竟案，盡沒入之，通家尚負責數巨萬。

translations.literal: Before long someone reported that Tong had smuggled coin-casting beyond the frontier passes; he was handed to officials for investigation, and there was quite a bit of evidence; the case was pushed to the end, everything was confiscated, and Tong's household still owed debts of several tens of millions.
translations.idiomatic: Soon he was accused of illegal minting beyond the frontier; the inquiry proved true, his property was seized, and he still owed tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 長公主賜鄧通，吏輒隨沒入之，一簪不得著身。

translations.literal: When the senior princess gave gifts to Deng Tong, the officials would follow and confiscate them, so that not a single hairpin could remain on his person.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the princess tried to help him with gifts, officials seized them before they reached him—not even a hairpin stayed his.

sentences.zh: 於是長公主乃令假衣食。

translations.literal: Thereupon the senior princess ordered that he be lent food and clothing.
translations.idiomatic: At last she could only arrange meager food and clothes on loan.

sentences.zh: 竟不得名一錢，寄死人家。

translations.literal: In the end he could not claim a single cash and died lodged in another's house.
translations.idiomatic: He died without a coin to his name, a guest in someone else's roof.

sentences.zh: 附：趙談、北宮伯子

translations.literal: Appendix: Zhao Tan, Beigong Bozi
translations.idiomatic: Appendix: Zhao Tan and Beigong Bozi

sentences.zh: 趙談者，以星氣幸，北宮伯子長者愛人，故親近，然皆不比鄧通。

translations.literal: Zhao Tan won favor through astrology; Beigong Bozi was a venerable man who loved people, and therefore was intimate with the ruler—yet neither compared to Deng Tong.
translations.idiomatic: Zhao Tan rose on star-charts and portents; Beigong Bozi, a kindly elder, stayed close to the throne—but neither matched Deng Tong's hold.

sentences.zh: 韓嫣字王孫，弓高侯穨當之孫也。

translations.literal: Han Yan, courtesy name Wangsun, was the grandson of Marquis of Gonggao Kui Dang.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan, styled Wangsun, was grandson of Marquis of Gonggao Kui Dang.

sentences.zh: 武帝為膠東王時，嫣與上學書相愛。

translations.literal: When Emperor Wu was Prince of Jiaodong, Yan studied writing with the sovereign and they loved each other.
translations.idiomatic: While Wu was still prince of Jiaodong, Han Yan studied with him and they grew inseparable.

sentences.zh: 及上為太子，愈益親嫣。

translations.literal: When the sovereign became heir apparent, he grew still closer to Yan.
translations.idiomatic: When Wu became heir apparent, the bond only tightened.

sentences.zh: 嫣善騎射，聰慧。

translations.literal: Yan was skilled at riding and shooting and was clever and quick.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan excelled at horsemanship and archery and was quick-witted.

sentences.zh: 上即位，欲事伐胡，而嫣先習兵，以故益尊貴，官至上大夫，賞賜擬鄧通。

translations.literal: When the sovereign took the throne he wished to campaign against the Hu, and Yan had already trained in arms; for this reason he grew more honored and noble, his office rose to senior grandee, and his rewards rivaled Deng Tong's.
translations.idiomatic: Once enthroned, Wu planned war on the Xiongnu; Han Yan, already versed in military matters, rose to senior grandee with largesse to rival Deng Tong's.

sentences.zh: 始時，嫣常與上共臥起。

translations.literal: At first Yan often slept and rose with the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: Early on he often shared the emperor's quarters.

sentences.zh: 江都王入朝，從上獵上林中。

translations.literal: The king of Jiangdu came to court and accompanied the sovereign on a hunt in Shanglin Park.
translations.idiomatic: The king of Jiangdu attended court and joined a hunt in Shanglin Park.

sentences.zh: 天子車駕蹕道未行，先使嫣乘副車，從數十百騎馳視獸。

translations.literal: The imperial train had not yet cleared the cordoned road when the sovereign first sent Yan in the secondary chariot, with several tens or hundreds of riders galloping ahead to scout the game.
translations.idiomatic: Before the main procession moved, Wu sent Han Yan ahead in the escort carriage with scores of horsemen to beat the cover.

sentences.zh: 江都王望見，以為天子，辟從者，伏謁道旁。

translations.literal: The king of Jiangdu saw this, thought it was the Son of Heaven, dismissed his attendants, and prostrated himself in obeisance beside the road.
translations.idiomatic: The king mistook the party for the emperor, sent his escort aside, and knelt by the road.

sentences.zh: 嫣驅不見。

translations.literal: Yan drove past without acknowledging him.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan raced past without a nod.

sentences.zh: 既過，江都王怒，為皇太后泣，請得歸國入宿衛，比韓嫣。

translations.literal: After he had passed, the king of Jiangdu was enraged, wept to the empress dowager, and begged to return to his state and enter palace guard duty, to be classed with Han Yan.
translations.idiomatic: Humiliated, the king wept to the empress dowager and asked to go home to his kingdom or take palace guard duty—anything but rank with Han Yan.

sentences.zh: 太后由此銜嫣。

translations.literal: From this the empress dowager bore a grudge against Yan.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager never forgave Han Yan.

sentences.zh: 嫣侍，出入永巷不禁，以奸聞皇太后。

translations.literal: When Yan attended, his comings and goings through the Eternal Lane were unrestricted, and he became known to the empress dowager for debauchery.
translations.idiomatic: Han Yan moved freely through the harem lanes, and word of his misconduct reached the empress dowager.

sentences.zh: 太后怒，使使賜嫣死。

translations.literal: The empress dowager was angry and sent an envoy to grant Yan death.
translations.idiomatic: She sent an order that he must die.

sentences.zh: 上為謝，終不能得，嫣遂死。

translations.literal: The sovereign pleaded for him but in the end could not obtain it, and Yan thereupon died.
translations.idiomatic: Wu begged in vain; Han Yan was put to death.

sentences.zh: 附：韓說

translations.literal: Appendix: Han Shuo
translations.idiomatic: Appendix: Han Shuo

sentences.zh: 嫣弟說，亦愛幸，以軍功封案道侯，巫蠱時為戾太子所殺。

translations.literal: Yan's younger brother Shuo was also beloved and favored; he was enfeoffed marquis of Andao for military merit and was killed by Prince Li during the witchcraft affair.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Han Shuo, another favorite, became marquis of Andao on merit and was killed by the heir apparent Li in the witchcraft scandal.

sentences.zh: 子增封龍雒侯、大司馬、車騎將軍，自有傳。

translations.literal: His son Zeng was enfeoffed marquis of Longluo, grand marshal, and chariot-and-cavalry general; he has his own biography.
translations.idiomatic: His son Han Zeng rose to marquis of Longluo, grand marshal, and chariot-and-cavalry general—he has a separate biography.

sentences.zh: 李延年

translations.literal: Li Yannian
translations.idiomatic: Li Yannian

sentences.zh: 李延年，中山人，身及父母兄弟皆故倡也。

translations.literal: Li Yannian was a man of Zhongshan; he and his parents and brothers had all been entertainers by profession.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yannian of Zhongshan came from a family of professional performers.

sentences.zh: 延年坐法腐刑，給事狗監中。

translations.literal: Yannian was sentenced by law to castration and served in the dog kennel.
translations.idiomatic: Convicted, he was castrated and assigned to the imperial dog kennel.

sentences.zh: 女弟得幸於上，號李夫人，列《外戚傳》。

translations.literal: His younger sister won the sovereign's favor and was titled Lady Li; she is listed in the "Consorts" biography.
translations.idiomatic: His sister became Lady Li, a favorite—her story is in the "Consorts" chapter.

sentences.zh: 延年善歌，為新變聲。

translations.literal: Yannian was skilled at singing and created new, altered tones.
translations.idiomatic: He was a superb singer who devised new vocal styles.

sentences.zh: 是時，上方興天地祠，欲造樂，令司馬相如等作詩頌。

translations.literal: At that time the sovereign was promoting sacrifices to Heaven and Earth and wished to create music, ordering Sima Xiangru and others to compose verse for hymns.
translations.idiomatic: Wu was founding state rituals to Heaven and Earth and wanted fresh ritual music; Sima Xiangru and others were told to supply hymn texts.

sentences.zh: 延年輒承意弦歌所造詩，為之新聲曲。

translations.literal: Yannian would take the intent and set the newly composed poems to strings and song, making new musical settings for them.
translations.idiomatic: Li Yannian set each new poem to melody and accompaniment.

sentences.zh: 而李夫人產昌邑王，延年由是貴為協律都尉，佩二千石印綬，而與上臥起，其愛幸埒韓嫣。

translations.literal: And when Lady Li bore the king of Changyi, Yannian for this reason became noble as director of harmony, wore the seal and ribbon of two thousand piculs, slept and rose with the sovereign, and his favor rivaled Han Yan's.
translations.idiomatic: When Lady Li bore the king of Changyi, he was made director of harmony with a two-thousand-picul seal, shared the emperor's intimacy, and matched Han Yan in favor.

sentences.zh: 久之，延年弟季與中人亂，出入驕恣。

translations.literal: After a long time Yannian's younger brother Ji consorted with palace attendants and came and went arrogant and unrestrained.
translations.idiomatic: In time his brother Ji took lovers among the eunuchs and swaggered through the palace.

sentences.zh: 及李夫人卒後，其愛弛，上遂誅延年兄弟宗族。

translations.literal: When Lady Li died afterward, his favor slackened, and the sovereign executed Yannian's brothers and entire clan.
translations.idiomatic: After Lady Li's death his star faded; Wu executed Li Yannian and his whole clan.

sentences.zh: 附：衛青、霍去病

translations.literal: Appendix: Wei Qing, Huo Qubing
translations.idiomatic: Appendix: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing

sentences.zh: 是後，寵臣大氐外戚之家也。

translations.literal: After this, favorites were for the most part from affinal families.
translations.idiomatic: From then on, "favorites" tended to be in-laws of the throne.

sentences.zh: 衛青、霍去病皆愛幸，然亦以功能自進。

translations.literal: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing were both beloved and favored, yet they also advanced themselves by merit and achievement.
translations.idiomatic: Wei Qing and Huo Qubing enjoyed imperial favor, but they also earned their place by real achievement.

sentences.zh: 石顯字君房，濟南人；

translations.literal: Shi Xian, courtesy name Junfang, was a man of Jinan;
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian, styled Junfang, was a native of Jinan;

sentences.zh: 弘恭，沛人也。

translations.literal: Hong Gong was a man of Pei.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Gong came from Pei.

sentences.zh: 皆少坐法腐刑，為中黃門，以選為中尚書。

translations.literal: Both in youth were sentenced by law to castration and became attendants of the yellow gates, then by selection became secretaries of the central secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: Both were castrated for crimes in youth, entered as yellow-gate attendants, and were chosen for the central secretariat.

sentences.zh: 宣帝時任中書官，恭明習法令故事，善為請奏，能稱其職。

translations.literal: In Xuandi's time they held central secretariat posts; Gong was thoroughly versed in statutes and precedents, skilled at drafting memorials, and able to fill his office.
translations.idiomatic: Under Xuandi they ran the inner chancellery; Hong Gong knew law and precedent cold and drafted petitions to perfection.

sentences.zh: 恭為令，顯為僕射。

translations.literal: Gong was director; Xian was vice director.
translations.idiomatic: Hong Gong was chief; Shi Xian, his deputy.

sentences.zh: 元帝即位數年，恭死，顯代為中書令。

translations.literal: Several years after Emperor Yuan took the throne, Gong died and Xian replaced him as director of the central secretariat.
translations.idiomatic: A few years into Yuan's reign Hong Gong died and Shi Xian became chief of the secretariat.

sentences.zh: 是時，元帝被疾，不親政事，方隆好於音樂，以顯久典事，中人無外黨，精專可信任，遂委以政。

translations.literal: At that time Emperor Yuan was afflicted with illness, did not personally handle government affairs, was then deeply fond of music; because Xian had long managed affairs, the inner attendants had no outside faction, were focused and trustworthy, and so government was entrusted to him.
translations.idiomatic: Yuan was often ill and left state business aside while he lost himself in music. Shi Xian, long trusted in the palace, had no outside clique and seemed safe—so power passed to him.

sentences.zh: 事無小大，因顯白決，貴幸傾朝，百僚皆敬事顯。

translations.literal: Matters large and small were decided on white slips through Xian; he was so honored and favored that he tilted the whole court, and the hundred officials all treated Xian with respect.
translations.idiomatic: Every decision, great or small, went through Shi Xian; the court leaned his way and every minister deferred to him.

sentences.zh: 顯為人巧慧習事，能探得人主微指，內深賊，持詭辯以中傷人，忤恨睚眥，輒被以危法。

translations.literal: Xian was by nature clever and practiced at affairs, could probe the ruler's subtle intentions, was inwardly deep and vicious, held specious argument to wound others, and for resentment over the slightest squint he would trap them with fatal law.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian was clever, read the emperor's slightest wish, and nursed a vicious grudge: one cross word could bring a capital charge framed in legal jargon.

sentences.zh: 初元中，前將軍蕭望之及光祿大夫周堪、宗正劉更生皆給事中。

translations.literal: In the Chuyuan era, former general Xiao Wangzhi, grand counsellor of the household Zhou Kan, and director of the imperial clan Liu Gengsheng all held concurrent appointment as palace attendants.
translations.idiomatic: During Chuyuan, Xiao Wangzhi, Zhou Kan, and Liu Gengsheng held joint appointments as palace attendants.

sentences.zh: 望之領尚書事，知顯專權邪辟，建白以為：「尚書百官之本，國家樞機，宜以通明公正處之。

translations.literal: Wangzhi headed secretariat affairs, knew Xian monopolized power in a crooked way, and submitted a memorial saying: "The secretariat is the root of the hundred offices and the pivot of the state; it should be staffed with enlightened, fair men.
translations.idiomatic: Xiao Wangzhi, heading the secretariat, saw Shi Xian's crooked monopoly and argued: "That office is the nerve center of government; it needs open, upright men.

sentences.zh: 武帝游宴後庭，故用宦者，非古制也。

translations.literal: Emperor Wu roamed and feasted in the rear palace, therefore used eunuchs—this is not the ancient system.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Wu used eunuchs there because he banqueted in the inner palace—but that was never classical practice.

sentences.zh: 宜罷中書宦官，應古不近刑人。

translations.literal: The central secretariat eunuchs should be abolished, conforming to antiquity of not keeping mutilated men close."
translations.idiomatic: Abolish eunuch secretaries and return to the old rule: do not keep the mutilated at the ruler's elbow."

sentences.zh: 」元帝不聽，由是大與顯忤。

translations.literal: " Emperor Yuan did not listen, and from this he greatly opposed Xian.
translations.idiomatic: " Yuan refused, and Xiao Wangzhi made a bitter enemy of Shi Xian.

sentences.zh: 後皆害焉，望之自殺，堪、更生廢錮，不得復進用，語在《望之傳》。

translations.literal: Afterward they were all harmed: Wangzhi killed himself; Kan and Gengsheng were dismissed and barred from further advancement—the account is in the "Wangzhi" biography.
translations.idiomatic: All were ruined: Xiao Wangzhi committed suicide; Zhou Kan and Liu Gengsheng were cashiered and blocked from office—the full story is in Wangzhi's biography.

sentences.zh: 後太中大夫張猛、魏郡太守京房、御史中丞陳咸、待詔賈捐之皆嘗奏封事，或召見，言顯短。

translations.literal: Later grand counsellor of the palace Zhang Meng, governor of Wei commandery Jing Fang, imperial secretary Chen Xian, and awaiting-orders Jia Juanzhi had all submitted sealed memorials or been summoned for audience to speak of Xian's faults.
translations.idiomatic: Later Zhang Meng, Jing Fang, Chen Xian, and Jia Juanzhi memorialized or were called in to attack Shi Xian.

sentences.zh: 顯求索其罪，房、捐之棄市，猛自殺於公車，咸抵罪，髡為城旦。

translations.literal: Xian sought out their crimes: Fang and Juanzhi were executed in the marketplace; Meng killed himself on the public carriage grounds; Chen Xian was convicted, shaved, and sentenced as wall dawn labor.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian hunted charges: Jing Fang and Jia Juanzhi died in the market; Zhang Meng killed himself at the carriage park; Chen Xian was shorn and sentenced as corvée labor.

sentences.zh: 及鄭令蘇建得顯私書奏之，後以它事論死。

translations.literal: When Magistrate of Zheng Su Jian obtained a private letter from Xian and presented it, he was later condemned to death on another charge.
translations.idiomatic: When Su Jian of Zheng presented a private letter from Shi Xian, he himself was later executed on another pretext.

sentences.zh: 自是公卿以下畏顯，重足一跡。

translations.literal: From this the ministers down to the gentlemen were afraid of Xian, standing as if on one footprint.
translations.idiomatic: After that, from ministers downward, everyone feared Shi Xian and scarcely dared breathe.

sentences.zh: 顯與中書僕射牢梁、少府五鹿充宗結為黨友，諸附倚者皆得寵位。

translations.literal: Xian formed a clique with vice director of the central secretariat Lao Liang and chamberlain for the national treasury Wulu Chongzong; those who attached themselves all obtained favored posts.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian allied with Lao Liang and Wulu Chongzong; hangers-on won fat offices.

sentences.zh: 民歌之曰：「牢邪石邪，五鹿客邪！

translations.literal: The people sang: "Lao or Shi, Wulu as guest!
translations.idiomatic: A ditty ran: "Lao or Shi, Wulu for company!

sentences.zh: 印何纍纍，綬若若邪！

translations.literal: " "How piled the seals, how long the ribbons!"
translations.idiomatic: " "Seals in stacks, ribbons trailing long!"

sentences.zh: 」言其兼官據勢也。

translations.literal: " —meaning they held concurrent offices and monopolized power.
translations.idiomatic: " —mocking their stacked titles and grip on power.

sentences.zh: 顯見左將軍馮奉世父子為公卿著名，女又為昭儀在內，顯心欲附之，薦言昭儀兄謁者逡修敕宜侍帷幄。

translations.literal: Xian saw that General of the Left Feng Fengshi and his sons were well known as ministers, and that his daughter was also a lady of handsome deportment in the inner palace; Xian wished to attach himself and recommended that the lady's elder brother, usher Jun, was cultivated and proper and should attend within the curtains.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing General of the Left Feng Fengshi and his sons prominent at court—and a daughter already a favored lady—Shi Xian tried to curry favor by recommending her brother, Usher Jun, as fit for inner service.

sentences.zh: 天子召見，欲以為侍中，逡請間言事。

translations.literal: The Son of Heaven summoned him for audience and wished to make him palace attendant; Jun requested a private word on affairs.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor summoned Jun for palace attendant; Jun asked for a private audience.

sentences.zh: 上聞逡言顯顓權，天子大怒，罷逡歸郎官。

translations.literal: When the sovereign heard Jun's words that Xian monopolized power, the Son of Heaven was greatly angry and dismissed Jun back to gentleman status.
translations.idiomatic: Jun denounced Shi Xian's monopoly; the emperor, furious, busted him back to ordinary gentleman.

sentences.zh: 其後御史大夫缺，群臣皆舉逡兄大鴻臚野王行能第一，天子以問顯，顯曰：「九卿無出野王者。

translations.literal: Afterward when the imperial counsellor was vacant, the ministers all recommended Jun's elder brother, grand herald Ye Wang, as first in conduct and ability; the Son of Heaven asked Xian, and Xian said: "Among the nine ministers none surpasses Ye Wang.
translations.idiomatic: When the imperial counsellor's post fell open, the court ranked Ye Wang, Jun's brother and grand herald, first in talent; the emperor consulted Shi Xian, who said, "No nine minister outranks Ye Wang.

sentences.zh: 然野王親昭儀兄，臣恐後世必以陛下度越眾賢，私後宮親以為三公。

translations.literal: " "Yet Ye Wang is the elder brother of the lady of handsome deportment by kinship; your servant fears later generations will say Your Majesty passed over the worthies to favor a harem connection for the three dukes."
translations.idiomatic: " Yet he is the favored lady's brother. I fear posterity will say you skipped better men to elevate a bedchamber connection to one of the three excellencies."

sentences.zh: 」上曰：「善，吾不見是。

translations.literal: " The sovereign said: "Good—I did not see that."
translations.idiomatic: " "Well said," the emperor replied. "I had not seen that."

sentences.zh: 」乃下詔嘉美野王，廢而不用，語在《野王傳》。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he issued an edict praising Ye Wang, set him aside, and did not employ him; the account is in the 《Ye Wang》 biography.
translations.idiomatic: He then published an edict praising Ye Wang while shelving him all the same; the full story is in the 《Biography of Ye Wang》.

sentences.zh: 顯內自知擅權事柄在掌握，恐天子一旦納用左右耳目，有以間己，乃時歸誠，取一信以為驗。

translations.literal: Xian inwardly knew he monopolized power and the levers of affairs were in his grasp; he feared that if the Son of Heaven one day took counsel from the eyes and ears at his side, there would be means to alienate him, so from time to time he feigned sincerity and sought one proof of trust.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian knew he held the real strings of government and dreaded the day the emperor might listen to other intimates; he staged moments of contrition to manufacture proof of loyalty.

sentences.zh: 顯嘗使至諸官有所征發，顯先自白，恐後漏盡宮門閉，請使詔吏開門。

translations.literal: Once when Xian had been sent to the various offices on requisition business, he first reported himself, fearing that afterward the clepsydra would run out and the palace gates close, and requested that an edict clerk be ordered to open the gate.
translations.idiomatic: On a requisition run to outside offices he filed notice in advance, pleading that the water-clock might expire and lock him out—so he asked for an authorized clerk to keep the gate open.

sentences.zh: 上許之。

translations.literal: The sovereign granted it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor agreed.

sentences.zh: 顯故投夜還，稱詔開門入。

translations.literal: Xian purposely delayed until night to return, claimed an edict, had the gate opened, and entered.
translations.idiomatic: He deliberately came back after dark, invoked imperial orders, and walked through.

sentences.zh: 後果有上書告顯顓命矯詔開宮門，天子聞之，笑以其書示顯。

translations.literal: Later someone did memorialize accusing Xian of monopolizing orders and forging an edict to open the palace gate; when the Son of Heaven heard it, he laughed and showed Xian the document.
translations.idiomatic: When a memorial later charged him with forging orders to force the gate, the emperor only laughed and handed Shi Xian the paper.

sentences.zh: 顯因泣曰：「陛下過私小臣，屬任以事，群下無不嫉妒欲陷害臣者，事類如此非一，唯獨明主知之。

translations.literal: Xian thereupon wept and said: "Your Majesty has wrongly favored your petty servant and entrusted him with affairs; none below are not jealous and wish to trap your servant; matters of this kind are not one alone—only an enlightened ruler knows it.
translations.idiomatic: Shi Xian wept: "You have over-favored a minor official and piled work on him; every man under heaven envies me and wants me ruined. This is not the first such accusation—only a clear-sighted sovereign sees through it."

sentences.zh: 愚臣微賤，誠不能以一軀稱快萬眾，任天下之怨，臣願歸樞機職，受後宮掃除之役，死無所恨，唯陛下哀憐財幸，以此全活小臣。

translations.literal: Your foolish servant is base and low, truly unable with one body to satisfy the myriad masses and bear the resentments of the realm; your servant wishes to resign the pivot office and accept menial sweeping duties in the rear palace—dying he would have no regret, only beg Your Majesty's pity and favor, thereby to preserve and spare your petty servant.
translations.idiomatic: I am too humble to please the multitude or absorb the world's hatred. Let me surrender the secretariat and sweep the inner palace instead—I would die content if Your Majesty would pity me and let me live."

sentences.zh: 」天子以為然而憐之，數勞勉顯，加厚賞賜，賞賜及賂遺訾一萬萬。

translations.literal: " The Son of Heaven thought it so and pitied him, repeatedly consoled and encouraged Xian, increased gifts and rewards; rewards, gifts, and bribes came to a hundred million cash.
translations.idiomatic: " Yuan bought the performance: he comforted Shi Xian again and again, heaped gifts on him, and cash from stipends and kickbacks reportedly reached a hundred million.

sentences.zh: 初，顯聞眾人匈匈，言己殺前將軍蕭望之。

translations.literal: Earlier, Xian had heard the crowd in an uproar, saying he had killed former general Xiao Wangzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Earlier, word on the street blamed Shi Xian for Xiao Wangzhi's death.

sentences.zh: 望之當世名儒，顯恐天下學士姍己，病之。

translations.literal: Wangzhi was a famous scholar of the age; Xian feared scholars of the realm would despise him and was distressed by it.
translations.idiomatic: Wangzhi had been the age's leading classicist; Shi Xian worried the literati would turn every pen against him.

sentences.zh: 是時，明經著節士琅邪貢禹為諫大夫，顯使人致意，深自結納。

translations.literal: At that time the classicist famed for integrity, Gong Yu of Langye, was grandee remonstrant; Xian sent someone to convey his intentions and bound himself closely to Yu.
translations.idiomatic: He courted Gong Yu of Langye—a noted scholar of integrity then serving as remonstrant—through intermediaries and tied him with favors.

sentences.zh: 顯因薦禹天子，歷位九卿，至御史大夫，禮事之甚備。

translations.literal: Xian therefore recommended Yu to the Son of Heaven; Yu passed through the nine ministers to imperial counsellor, and Xian treated him with full ceremony.
translations.idiomatic: He pushed Gong Yu up the ladder to the nine ministers and even to imperial counsellor, honoring him with elaborate courtesy.

sentences.zh: 議者於是稱顯，以為不妒譖望之矣。

translations.literal: Commentators then praised Xian, thinking he no longer envied and slandered Wangzhi.
translations.idiomatic: Gossip then called Shi Xian magnanimous, as if he had never plotted against Wangzhi.

sentences.zh: 顯之設變詐以自解免取信人主者，皆此類也。

translations.literal: The schemes and deceptions Xian contrived to extricate himself and win the ruler's trust were all of this sort.
translations.idiomatic: Every ruse he used to save his skin and keep the emperor's confidence looked like this.

sentences.zh: 淳于長

translations.literal: Chunyu Chang
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Chang

sentences.zh: 淳于長字子鴻，魏郡元城人也。

translations.literal: Chunyu Chang, courtesy name Zihong, was a man of Yuancheng in Wei commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Chang, styled Zihong, came from Yuancheng in Wei commandery.

sentences.zh: 少以太后姊子為黃門郎，未進幸。

translations.literal: In youth he became a gentleman of the yellow gate as the empress dowager's sister's son, and had not yet advanced to favor.
translations.idiomatic: Through his tie to the empress dowager's sister he entered as a yellow-gate gentleman before he had won the throne's notice.

sentences.zh: 會大將軍王鳳病，長侍病，晨夜扶丞左右，甚為甥舅之恩。

translations.literal: When Grand General Wang Feng fell ill, Chang nursed him, supporting him morning and night at his side—the kindness of nephew and uncle ran very deep.
translations.idiomatic: When Wang Feng fell ill, Chang kept vigil day and night at his bedside—the bond of uncle and nephew ran deep.

sentences.zh: 鳳且終，以長屬托太后及帝。

translations.literal: When Feng was about to die, he entrusted Chang to the empress dowager and the emperor.
translations.idiomatic: On his deathbed Wang Feng commended Chang to the empress dowager and the emperor.

sentences.zh: 帝嘉長義，拜為列校尉諸曹，遷水衡都尉侍中，至衛尉九卿。

translations.literal: The emperor praised Chang's devotion, appointed him to various column commandant posts, promoted him to chief commandant of waters and forests and palace attendant, and raised him to defender of the capital among the nine ministers.
translations.idiomatic: Chengdi praised his loyalty, moved him through column commandant posts to chief commandant of waters and forests and palace attendant, then to defender of the capital.

sentences.zh: 久之，趙飛燕貴幸，上欲立以為皇后，太后以其所出微，難之。

translations.literal: After a long time Zhao Feiyan was honored and favored; the sovereign wished to establish her as empress, but the empress dowager, because her birth was lowly, made difficulties.
translations.idiomatic: When Zhao Feiyan rose high enough to aim at the empress's throne, the empress dowager balked at her low birth.

sentences.zh: 長主往來通語東宮。

translations.literal: The senior princess went back and forth conveying words to the eastern palace.
translations.idiomatic: The senior princess shuttled messages to the empress dowager's residence.

sentences.zh: 歲餘，趙皇后得立，上甚德之，乃追顯長前功，下詔曰：「前將作大匠解萬年奏請營作昌陵，罷弊海內，侍中衛尉長數白宜止徙家反故處，朕以長言下公卿，議者皆合長計。

translations.literal: After more than a year Empress Zhao was established; the sovereign was deeply grateful and therefore brought forward Chang's earlier merit, issuing an edict saying: "Former director of works Xie Wannian memorialized to build Changling, exhausting the realm; palace attendant and defender of the capital Chang repeatedly stated that work should stop and households be moved back to their old sites. We laid Chang's words before the dukes and ministers, and those who deliberated all agreed with Chang's plan.
translations.idiomatic: More than a year later the Zhao empress was installed, and Chengdi remembered the debt. He highlighted Chang's earlier service with an edict: when Director of Works Xie Wannian had pushed the Changling project to the empire's ruin, Chang—as palace attendant and defender—had urged halting the move and sending families home. The court had debated his proposal and endorsed it.

sentences.zh: 首建至策，民以康寧。

translations.literal: He first proposed the supremely right policy, and the people thereby had peace.
translations.idiomatic: That first, sound plan restored calm to the common people.

sentences.zh: 其賜長爵關內侯。

translations.literal: Let Chang be granted the rank of marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: He awarded Chang a marquis-within-the-passes title.

sentences.zh: 」後遂封為定陵侯，大見信用，貴傾公卿。

translations.literal: " Thereafter he was enfeoffed marquis of Dingling, greatly trusted, his eminence tilting the dukes and ministers.
translations.idiomatic: " Chang was soon made marquis of Dingling, deeply trusted, and his prestige overshadowed the high ministers.

sentences.zh: 外交諸侯牧守，賂遺賞賜亦累巨萬。

translations.literal: He had external dealings with marquises, governors, and shepherds; bribes, gifts, and rewards likewise piled to hundreds of thousands.
translations.idiomatic: He traded favors with regional lords and governors; gifts and graft ran into the millions.

sentences.zh: 多畜妻妾，淫於聲色，不奉法度。

translations.literal: He kept many wives and concubines, debauched himself in music and women, and did not observe the laws.
translations.idiomatic: He hoarded concubines, drowned himself in music and women, and treated the statutes with contempt.

sentences.zh: 初，許皇后坐執左道廢處長定宮，而後姊靡為龍額思侯夫人，寡居。

translations.literal: Earlier, Empress Xu had been deposed for practising unorthodox arts and was confined to the Palace of Lasting Settlement; her elder sister Mi was wife of the marquis of Longesi and lived widowed.
translations.idiomatic: Former Empress Xu had lost her title for "left-path" sorcery and lived at the Changding Palace; her sister Mi, widow of the marquis of Longesi, kept house alone.

sentences.zh: 長與靡私通，因取為小妻。

translations.literal: Chang had illicit relations with Mi and took her as a junior wife.
translations.idiomatic: Chunyu Chang took Mi as a secret concubine.

sentences.zh: 許后因靡賂遺長，欲求復為婕妤。

translations.literal: The former empress Xu, through Mi, bribed Chang, seeking to be restored as lady of handsome favor.
translations.idiomatic: The deposed empress sent gifts through Mi, begging to be restored as a lady of handsome favor.

sentences.zh: 長受許后金錢乘輿服御物前後千餘萬，詐許為白上，立以為左皇后。

translations.literal: Chang received from Empress Xu over ten million in cash, carriages, and wardrobe items over time, falsely promised to speak to the sovereign for her, and said he would establish her as empress to the left.
translations.idiomatic: Chang pocketed more than ten million in coin, carriages, and wardrobe from her, promised to plead her case, and swore he would make her a "left empress."

sentences.zh: 靡每入長定宮，輒與靡書，戲侮許后，嫚易無不言。

translations.literal: Whenever Mi entered the Palace of Lasting Settlement, he would write letters to Mi, mocking and humiliating the former empress Xu, leaving no insult unsaid.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever Mi visited the Changding Palace, he sent letters along with her, ridiculing the former empress in the crudest terms.

sentences.zh: 交通書記，賂遺連年。

translations.literal: They kept up correspondence; bribes continued year after year.
translations.idiomatic: The letters and bribes flowed without stop for years.

sentences.zh: 是時，帝舅曲陽侯王根為大司馬票騎將軍，輔政數歲，久病，數乞骸骨。

translations.literal: At that time the emperor's maternal uncle Marquis of Quyang Wang Gen was grand marshal and general-in-chief of agile cavalry, assisting government several years, chronically ill, repeatedly begging to retire.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor's uncle Wang Gen, marquis of Quyang, had served as regent grand marshal and general of agile cavalry for years; chronic illness drove him to ask for retirement.

sentences.zh: 長以外親居九卿位，次第當代根。

translations.literal: Chang, as affinal kin, held a nine-minister post and in order was next to replace Gen.
translations.idiomatic: As an in-law already in the nine ministers, Chang stood next in line to succeed him.

sentences.zh: 根兄子新都侯王莽心害長寵，私聞長取許靡，受長定宮賂遺。

translations.literal: Wang Mang, marquis of Xindu and nephew of Gen, inwardly resented Chang's favor and privately learned that Chang had taken Xu Mi and received bribes from the Palace of Lasting Settlement.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang of Xindu, Wang Gen's nephew, envied Chang's rise and quietly confirmed his affair with Xu Mi and the bribes from Changding.

sentences.zh: 莽侍曲陽侯疾，因言：「長見將軍久病，意喜，自以當代輔政，至對衣冠議語署置。

translations.literal: While attending Quyang's illness, Mang said: "Chang sees the general has been ill long, is pleased at heart, thinks he will replace you as regent, and even discusses with men in caps and robes appointments in the yamen."
translations.idiomatic: At Wang Gen's sickbed Mang whispered that Chang was counting the days until he could take the regency and was already handing out jobs in the ministries.

sentences.zh: 」具言其罪過。

translations.literal: " He detailed his crimes.
translations.idiomatic: " He laid out the counts in full.

sentences.zh: 根怒曰：「即如是，何不白也？

translations.literal: " Gen angrily said: "If so, why did you not report it?"
translations.idiomatic: " Wang Gen snapped, "If that is true, why have you kept silent?"

sentences.zh: 」莽曰：「未知將軍意，故未敢言。

translations.literal: " Mang said: "I did not know the general's mind, therefore dared not speak."
translations.idiomatic: " "I was not sure where you stood," Mang replied.

sentences.zh: 」根曰：「趣白東宮。

translations.literal: " Gen said: "Hurry and report to the eastern palace."
translations.idiomatic: " "Go to the empress dowager at once," Wang Gen ordered.

sentences.zh: 」莽求見太后，具言長驕佚，欲代曲陽侯，對莽母上車，私與長定貴人姊通，受取其衣物。

translations.literal: " Mang sought audience with the empress dowager and fully said Chang was arrogant and dissolute, wished to replace the Quyang marquis, had helped Mang's mother into a carriage in a presumptuous way, had private relations with the elder sister of the Changding lady of honor, and received her clothing.
translations.idiomatic: " Mang told the empress dowager everything: Chang's arrogance, his designs on Wang Gen's post, his brazen familiarity with Mang's mother at the carriage step, his affair with the Changding noblewoman's sister, and the gifts he took from her."

sentences.zh: 太后亦怒曰：「兒至如此！

translations.literal: " The empress dowager also angrily said: "The son has come to this!"
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager snapped, "Has the boy sunk this low?"

sentences.zh: 往白之帝！

translations.literal: " Go report him to the emperor!"
translations.idiomatic: " "Tell the emperor immediately."

sentences.zh: 」莽白上，上乃免長官，遣就國。

translations.literal: " Mang reported to the sovereign; the sovereign thereupon dismissed Chang from office and sent him to his state.
translations.idiomatic: " Mang carried the word to Chengdi, who stripped Chang of his posts and ordered him to his fief.

sentences.zh: 初，長為侍中，奉兩宮使，親密。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Chang was palace attendant, he carried missions for both palaces and was intimate.
translations.idiomatic: In his days as palace attendant he had run errands for both palaces and been treated as family.

sentences.zh: 紅陽侯立獨不得為大司馬輔政，立自疑為長毀譖，常怨毒長。

translations.literal: Marquis of Hongyang Wang Li alone did not obtain appointment as grand marshal to assist government; Li suspected himself slandered by Chang and constantly bore bitter hatred.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Li, marquis of Hongyang, alone had been passed over for the regency; he was convinced Chang had slandered him and nursed a murderous grudge.

sentences.zh: 上知之。

translations.literal: The sovereign knew it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor knew the bad blood.

sentences.zh: 及長當就國也，立嗣子融從長請車騎，長以珍寶因融重遺立，立因為長言。

translations.literal: When Chang was to go to his state, Li's heir son Rong requested chariotry from Chang; Chang sent treasures through Rong as a heavy gift to Li, and Li then spoke for Chang.
translations.idiomatic: When Chang prepared to leave the capital, Wang Li's heir Rong asked him for horses and carriages; Chang funneled rare gifts through Rong, and Wang Li put in a good word.

sentences.zh: 於是天子疑焉，下有司案驗。

translations.literal: Thereupon the Son of Heaven was suspicious and sent the case down to officials for investigation.
translations.idiomatic: The arrangement struck Chengdi as wrong; he handed the matter to the judiciary.

sentences.zh: 史捕融，立令融自殺以滅口。

translations.literal: The clerks arrested Rong; Li ordered Rong to kill himself to silence mouths.
translations.idiomatic: Clerks seized Rong; Wang Li ordered his own son to commit suicide to seal his lips.

sentences.zh: 上愈疑其有大奸，遂逮長系洛陽詔獄窮治。

translations.literal: The sovereign grew more suspicious of great villainy, thereupon arrested Chang and bound him in the Luoyang edict prison for exhaustive inquiry.
translations.idiomatic: That only deepened the emperor's suspicion of a vast conspiracy; Chang was jailed under imperial warrant at Luoyang and interrogated without mercy.

sentences.zh: 長具服戲侮長定宮，謀立左皇后，罪至大逆，死獄中。

translations.literal: Chang confessed fully to mocking the Palace of Lasting Settlement and plotting to establish a left empress—the crime reached great treason; he died in prison.
translations.idiomatic: He admitted to humiliating the Changding Palace and plotting a rival empress—capital treason—and died in custody.

sentences.zh: 妻子當坐者徙合浦，母若歸故郡。

translations.literal: Wives and children who should sit for the crime were banished to Hepu; his mother was allowed to return to her old commandery.
translations.idiomatic: Wives and children liable under the law went to Hepu; his mother was sent home to her native commandery.

sentences.zh: 紅陽侯立就國。

translations.literal: Marquis of Hongyang Li went to his state.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Li, marquis of Hongyang, was packed off to his fief as well.

sentences.zh: 將軍、卿、大夫、郡守坐長免罷者數十人。

translations.literal: Generals, ministers, grandees, and governors who sat for Chang and were dismissed numbered several tens.
translations.idiomatic: Several score generals, ministers, grandees, and governors fell with him.

sentences.zh: 莽遂代根為大司馬。

translations.literal: Mang thereupon replaced Gen as grand marshal.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Mang stepped into Wang Gen's place as grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 久之，還長母及子酺於長安。

translations.literal: After a long time (the court) allowed Chang's mother and son Pu to return to Chang'an.
translations.idiomatic: Years later his mother and son Pu were allowed back to the capital.

sentences.zh: 後酺有罪，莽復殺之，徙其家屬歸故郡。

translations.literal: Later Pu committed a crime; Mang again killed him and moved his household back to their old commandery.
translations.idiomatic: When Pu offended the law, Mang had him executed and exiled the rest of the family again.

sentences.zh: 附：張放

translations.literal: Appendix: Zhang Fang
translations.idiomatic: Appendix: Zhang Fang

sentences.zh: 始，長以外親親近，其愛幸不及富平侯張放。

translations.literal: At first Chang through affinal kinship had been close; his favor could not match Marquis of Fuping Zhang Fang.
translations.idiomatic: Even at his height, Chang's intimacy never matched that of Zhang Fang, marquis of Fuping.

sentences.zh: 放常與上臥起，俱為微行出入。

translations.literal: Fang often slept and rose with the sovereign; both went in disguise on outings.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Fang shared the emperor's bedchamber and roamed the city incognito at his side.

sentences.zh: 董賢字聖卿，雲陽人也。

translations.literal: Dong Xian, courtesy name Shengqing, was a man of Yunyang.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian, styled Shengqing, was a native of Yunyang.

sentences.zh: 父恭，為御史，任賢為太子舍人。

translations.literal: His father Gong was an imperial secretary; on Gong's recommendation Xian became gentleman of the household to the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: His father Dong Gong served as imperial secretary and used his privilege to appoint Xian gentleman of the household to the crown prince.

sentences.zh: 哀帝立，賢隨太子官為郎。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ai took the throne, Xian followed the heir apparent's establishment and became a gentleman.
translations.idiomatic: When Ai ascended, Xian moved from the heir's staff into the corps of gentlemen.

sentences.zh: 二歲餘，賢傳漏在殿下，為人美麗自喜，哀帝望見，說其儀貌，識而問之，曰：「是舍人董賢邪？

translations.literal: After more than two years Xian was on clepsydra duty below the hall; he was handsome and pleased with himself; Emperor Ai saw him from afar, delighted in his bearing, recognized him and asked, saying: "Is that not the household gentleman Dong Xian?"
translations.idiomatic: Two years later, while keeping the water-clock below the hall, he caught Ai's eye—handsome, self-possessed. The emperor recognized him and called out, "Is that not Gentleman Dong Xian?"

sentences.zh: 」因引上與語，拜為黃門郎，由是始幸。

translations.literal: " Thereupon he drew him up and spoke with him, appointed him gentleman of the yellow gate, and from this favor began.
translations.idiomatic: " He had him brought up for conversation, named him a yellow-gate gentleman, and favor began that day."

sentences.zh: 問及其父為雲中侯，即日徵為霸陵令，遷光祿大夫。

translations.literal: He asked about his father being marquis of Yunzhong; that very day he summoned him as magistrate of Baling, then promoted him to grand counsellor of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Learning that his father held the Yunzhong marquisate, Ai summoned the elder Dong the same day to be magistrate of Baling, then promoted him to grand counsellor of the household.

sentences.zh: 賢寵愛日甚，為駙馬都尉侍中，出則參乘，入御左右，旬月間賞賜累巨萬，貴震朝廷。

translations.literal: Xian's favor grew daily; he became commandant of the imperial son-in-law and palace attendant, rode as guard in the secondary chariot when going out and attended at the ruler's side when entering; within ten-day months rewards piled to millions, his eminence shaking the court.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian rose to commandant of the imperial son-in-law and palace attendant, rode escort when the emperor went abroad, and stayed at his elbow within; in weeks the gifts ran to millions and his power shook the court.

sentences.zh: 常與上臥起。

translations.literal: He often slept and rose with the sovereign.
translations.idiomatic: He often shared the emperor's couch.

sentences.zh: 嘗晝寢，偏藉上袖，上欲起，賢未覺，不欲動賢，乃斷袖而起。

translations.literal: Once during a daytime nap he lay partly on the sovereign's sleeve; the sovereign wished to rise but Xian had not awakened, and not wishing to disturb Xian, he cut the sleeve and rose.
translations.idiomatic: Once Ai napped with him; Xian lay across his sleeve. Rather than wake him, Ai took a knife and cut the cloth free.

sentences.zh: 其恩愛至此。

translations.literal: Such was the degree of their affection.
translations.idiomatic: That was the depth of their bond.

sentences.zh: 賢亦性柔和便辟，善為媚以自固。

translations.literal: Xian was also by nature gentle and yielding, clever at ingratiation, skilled at flattery to secure himself.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian was soft, pliant, and expert at ingratiating flattery.

sentences.zh: 每賜洗沐，不肯出，常留中視醫藥。

translations.literal: Whenever granted leave to bathe he would not go out, but constantly remained within to oversee medicine.
translations.idiomatic: Even on bath-leave he stayed inside to "supervise" the imperial physic.

sentences.zh: 上以賢難歸，詔令賢妻得通引籍殿中，止賢廬，若吏妻子居官寺捨。

translations.literal: Because the sovereign found it hard for Xian to return home, he edicted that Xian's wife might hold passage credentials into the palace and lodge in Xian's quarters, like the wife and children of an official dwelling in a government hostel.
translations.idiomatic: To spare him trips home, Ai let Dong Xian's wife carry palace passes and live in his lodge inside, as if she were quartered in a ministry hostel.

sentences.zh: 又召賢女弟以為昭儀，位次皇后，更名其捨為椒風，以配椒房雲。

translations.literal: He also summoned Xian's younger sister to be lady of handsome deportment, ranking next to the empress, renamed her residence Jiaofeng to pair with the Jiaofang (empress's) quarters.
translations.idiomatic: He took Dong Xian's sister as a lady of handsome deportment second only to the empress and renamed her hall Jiaofeng to echo the empress's Jiaofang.

sentences.zh: 昭儀及賢與妻旦夕上下，並侍左右。

translations.literal: The lady of handsome deportment and Xian and his wife attended morning and evening above and below, all serving at the ruler's side.
translations.idiomatic: The sister, Dong Xian, and his wife moved in a pack, attending the emperor morning and night.

sentences.zh: 賞賜昭儀及賢妻亦各千萬數。

translations.literal: Rewards to the lady of handsome deportment and Xian's wife likewise reached tens of millions each.
translations.idiomatic: The sister and the wife each received gifts in the tens of millions.

sentences.zh: 遷賢父為少府，賜爵關內侯，食邑，復徙為衛尉。

translations.literal: He promoted Xian's father to chamberlain for the palace revenues, granted him the rank of marquis within the passes with a fief, then transferred him to defender of the capital.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Gong was raised to chamberlain for the palace revenues, given a marquis-within-the-passes title with income, then shifted to defender of the capital.

sentences.zh: 又以賢妻父為將作大匠，弟為執金吾。

translations.literal: He also made Xian's father-in-law director of works and Xian's younger brother chief of the capital guard.
translations.idiomatic: His father-in-law became director of works; his brother, chief of the capital guard.

sentences.zh: 詔將作大匠為賢起大第北闕下，重殿洞門，木土之功窮極技巧，柱檻衣以綈錦。

translations.literal: He ordered the director of works to build for Xian a great mansion below the northern gate tower, with double halls and communicating doors; the carpentry and earthworks exhausted the utmost craft, pillars and balustrades draped in damask.
translations.idiomatic: The director of works raised a mansion north of the palace gate—stacked halls, linked gateways, joinery and masonry pushed to the limit, pillars wrapped in brocade.

sentences.zh: 下至賢家僮僕皆受上賜，及武庫禁兵，上方珍寶。

translations.literal: Down to the domestic servants of Xian's household all received gifts from the sovereign, including weapons from the arsenal and treasures from the imperial workshops.
translations.idiomatic: Even Dong Xian's house servants received imperial largesse—arms from the state arsenal and curios from the palace workshops.

sentences.zh: 其選物上弟盡在董氏，而乘輿所服乃其副也。

translations.literal: The finest selections from the top grade of tribute all went to the Dong clan, while what the imperial equipage wore were only the seconds.
translations.idiomatic: First pick of every tribute shipment went to the Dongs; the throne made do with what was left.

sentences.zh: 及至東園秘器，珠襦玉柙，豫以賜賢，無不備具。

translations.literal: As for the eastern garden secret coffin gear, the pearl shroud and jade casings, he pre-bestowed them on Xian, omitting nothing.
translations.idiomatic: Even the eastern-garden burial suit—pearl shroud, jade coffin panels—was signed over to Dong Xian in advance, nothing missing.

sentences.zh: 又令將作為賢起塚塋義陵旁，內為便房，剛柏題湊，外為徼道，周垣數里，門闕罘罳甚盛。

translations.literal: He also ordered the director of works to raise tombs and a burial park beside Yiling, with inner convenience chambers, stiff cypress head-block, outer patrol roads, a perimeter wall several li, and gate towers with heavy screens, very grand.
translations.idiomatic: He had a mortuary park built beside Ai's own tomb mound—inner chambers, cypress outer coffin, patrol paths, miles of wall, and ornate gate towers.

sentences.zh: 上欲侯賢而未有緣。

translations.literal: The sovereign wished to enfeoff Xian as marquis but had no pretext.
translations.idiomatic: Ai wanted to make Dong Xian a full marquis but lacked an excuse.

sentences.zh: 會待詔孫寵、息夫躬等告東平王雲後謁祠祀祝詛，下有司治，皆伏其辜。

translations.literal: It happened that awaiting-orders Sun Chong, Xi Fugong, and others reported that the queen of the king of Dongping, Yun, had visited shrines to curse by prayer; the case was sent down to officials for trial, and all confessed their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Then Sun Chong, Xi Fugong, and other awaiting-orders men denounced the king of Dongping's queen for shrine-cursing; the courts tried the case and extracted confessions.

sentences.zh: 上於是令躬、寵為因賢告東平事者，乃以其功下詔封賢為高安侯，躬宜陵侯，寵方陽侯，食邑各千戶。

translations.literal: The sovereign thereupon ordered Fugong and Chong to be the men who through Xian had reported the Dongping affair; on their merit he issued an edict enfeoffing Xian as marquis of Gao'an, Fugong as marquis of Yiling, and Chong as marquis of Fangyang, each with a thousand-household fief.
translations.idiomatic: Ai rewrote the story: Xi and Sun had exposed the plot "through" Dong Xian. He credited that tale and enfeoffed Dong Xian as marquis of Gao'an, Xi as marquis of Yiling, and Sun as marquis of Fangyang, each for a thousand households.

sentences.zh: 頃之，復益封賢二千戶。

translations.literal: Before long he again added two thousand households to Xian's fief.
translations.idiomatic: Soon another two thousand households were added to Dong Xian's appanage.

sentences.zh: 丞相王嘉內疑東平事冤，甚惡躬等，數諫爭，以賢為亂國制度，嘉竟坐言事下獄死。

translations.literal: Chancellor Wang Jia inwardly suspected the Dongping affair was unjust and deeply hated Fugong and the others; he remonstrated repeatedly, holding that Xian was disordering the institutions of state; Jia in the end sat for his remonstrance, was imprisoned, and died.
translations.idiomatic: Chancellor Wang Jia believed the Dongping case a frame job and loathed Xi Fugong's clique; he protested that Dong Xian was wrecking the polity—and died in prison for speaking out.

sentences.zh: 上初即位，祖母傅太后、母丁太后皆在，兩家先貴。

translations.literal: When the sovereign first took the throne, his grandmother Grand Empress Dowager Fu and his mother Empress Dowager Ding were both alive, and both families had long been eminent.
translations.idiomatic: When Ai first ascended, his grandmother, Grand Empress Dowager Fu, and his mother, Empress Dowager Ding, were still living, and both the Fu and Ding kindreds already held great power.

sentences.zh: 傅太后從弟喜先為大司馬輔政，數諫，失太后指，免官。

translations.literal: Grand Empress Dowager Fu's younger male cousin Xi had earlier been grand marshal assisting government; he remonstrated several times, missed the empress dowager's intent, and was dismissed.
translations.idiomatic: Fu Xi, a cousin of the grand empress dowager on the Fu side, had served as regent grand marshal; after repeated remonstrance that crossed her wishes, he was cashiered.

sentences.zh: 上舅丁明代為大司馬，亦任職，頗害賢寵，及丞相王嘉死，明甚憐之。

translations.literal: The sovereign's maternal uncle Ding Ming replaced him as grand marshal and also held office, somewhat harming Xian's favor; when Chancellor Wang Jia died, Ming greatly pitied him.
translations.idiomatic: Ai's uncle Ding Ming succeeded him as grand marshal and checked Dong Xian's influence somewhat; when Wang Jia died, Ding Ming grieved openly.

sentences.zh: 上浸重賢，欲極其位，而恨明如此，遂冊免明曰：「前東平王雲貪慾上位，祠祭祝詛，雲後舅伍宏以醫待詔，與校秘書郎楊閎結謀反逆，禍甚迫切。

translations.literal: The sovereign gradually valued Xian more and wished to raise him to the utmost position, but resented Ming for this; thereupon he issued a rescript dismissing Ming, saying: "Formerly the king of Dongping, Yun, coveted the high position, sacrificed and prayed curses; Yun's maternal uncle by the queen, Wu Hong, awaiting orders as a physician, plotted rebellion with collator-secretary Yang Hong—the harm was pressing and urgent.
translations.idiomatic: Ai came to prize Dong Xian above all and resented Ding Ming's obstruction. He cashiered him with a long indictment: Dongping King Yun had schemed for the throne with curses at the shrines; Yun's uncle Wu Hong, a physician-in-waiting, had conspired with collator Yang Hong—the peril had been immediate.

sentences.zh: 賴宗廟神靈，董賢等以聞，咸伏其辜。

translations.literal: Thanks to the spirits of the ancestral temples, Dong Xian and others reported it, and all confessed their guilt.
translations.idiomatic: Only the ancestors' blessing and Dong Xian's memorial had exposed the plot and brought confessions.

sentences.zh: 將軍從弟侍中奉車都尉吳、族父左曹屯騎校尉宣皆知宏及栩丹諸侯王后親，而宣除用丹為御屬，吳與宏交通厚善，數稱薦宏。

translations.literal: The general's younger male cousin by patriline, palace attendant and chief commandant of the imperial convoy Wu, and his clansman senior by generation, left aide colonel of garrison cavalry Xuan, both knew of Hong's and Xudan's kinship ties to marquises, kings, and empresses—yet Xuan appointed Dan as imperial household subordinate, Wu had thick dealings with Hong and repeatedly praised and recommended Hong.
translations.idiomatic: Ding Ming's paternal cousin Ding Wu—palace attendant and chief commandant of the convoy—and his senior clansman Ding Xuan, colonel of garrison cavalry, both knew Wu Hong and Wang Xudan were tied by marriage to the royal houses; still Ding Xuan put Wang Dan on his household staff, while Ding Wu traded favors with Hong and kept recommending him at court.

sentences.zh: 宏以附吳得興其噁心，因醫技進，幾危社稷，朕以恭皇后故，不忍有雲。

translations.literal: Hong by attaching to Wu could advance his evil designs, entered through medical skill, nearly endangered the altars of state; We, for the sake of the late Empress Gong, could not bear to punish Yun.
translations.idiomatic: Through Ding Wu's patronage Hong nursed treason under cover of physic and nearly overturned the dynasty; for the late Empress Gong's sake We spared the king of Dongping at the time.

sentences.zh: 將軍位尊任重，既不能明威立義，折消未萌，又不深疾雲、宏之惡，而懷非君上，阿為宣、吳，反痛恨雲等揚言為群下所冤，又親見言伍宏善醫，死可惜也，賢等獲封極幸。

translations.literal: " "You stand high with heavy charge, yet could neither display awe and establish right to quash evil in the bud, nor deeply hate the crimes of Yun and Hong, but harbored disloyalty to your ruler, sided with Xuan and Wu, and on the contrary bitterly resented Yun and the others while claiming the crowd below was wronged; you even said in person that Wu Hong was skilled in medicine and it was a pity he died, while Xian and others received enfeoffment beyond measure.
translations.idiomatic: " Yet you, grand marshal, neither stamped out the plot early nor condemned Yun and Hong; instead you harbored disloyalty, covered for Ding Xuan and Ding Wu, railed that Yun's faction had been slandered by the court, and openly mourned Wu Hong as a fine physician whose death was a waste—all while Dong Xian and his allies pocketed rewards you called excessive."

sentences.zh: 嫉妒忠良，非毀有功，於戲傷哉！

translations.literal: Jealous of the loyal and good, slandering those with achievements—alas, how grievous!
translations.idiomatic: To envy loyal ministers and tear down men of merit—how bitter a sight!

sentences.zh: 蓋『君親無將，將而誅之』。

translations.literal: For "against lord and father there must be no plotting; who plots is put to death."
translations.idiomatic: The classic rule runs: "No one may raise a hand against his ruler or his father; if he does, he dies for it."

sentences.zh: 是以季友鴆叔牙，《春秋》賢之；

translations.literal: Therefore Jiyou poisoned Shuya, and the 《Spring and Autumn》 deemed him worthy;
translations.idiomatic: So when Jiyou removed Shuya with poison, the 《Spring and Autumn》 praised him for it;

sentences.zh: 趙盾不討賊，謂之弒君。

translations.literal: Zhao Dun failed to punish the assassin and was called regicide.
translations.idiomatic: when Zhao Dun let the killer go free, the same annals branded him a king-slayer.

sentences.zh: 朕閔將軍陷於重刑，故以書飭。

translations.literal: We pity the general for falling into grave punishment, therefore rebuke him in writing.
translations.idiomatic: We grieve to see you, General, face the heaviest penalties, and send this written warning.

sentences.zh: 將軍遂非不改，復與丞相嘉相比，令嘉有依，得以罔上。

translations.literal: The general clings to error without reform, again bands with Chancellor Jia and gives Jia something to lean on, so that he can deceive the throne.
translations.idiomatic: You double down on your faults, stand shoulder to shoulder with Chancellor Wang Jia, and give him cover to mislead Us again.

sentences.zh: 有司致法將軍請獄治，朕惟噬膚之恩未忍，其上票騎將軍印綬，罷歸就第。

translations.literal: The authorities would apply the law and ask that the general be jailed for trial; We, mindful of the bond of flesh once shared, cannot bear it—surrender the seals and ribbons of general-in-chief of agile cavalry, be dismissed, and return to your residence.
translations.idiomatic: The law would send you to prison; remembering kinship once sealed in blood, We cannot—return the grand marshal's agile-cavalry seals, step down, and go home to your mansion.

sentences.zh: 」遂以賢代明為大司馬衛將軍。

translations.literal: " Thereupon Xian replaced Ming as grand marshal and general of the guards.
translations.idiomatic: " Dong Xian was named to replace Ding Ming as grand marshal and general of the guards."

sentences.zh: 冊曰：「朕承天序，惟稽古建爾於公，以為漢輔。

translations.literal: The rescript said: "We inheriting heaven's sequence, only by examining antiquity establish you as duke, to be a pillar of Han.
translations.idiomatic: The appointment scroll read: "We who inherit the mandate of heaven, following the ancient model, raise you to high duke as bulwark of the Han."

sentences.zh: 往悉爾心，統辟元戎，折衝綏遠，匡正庶事，允執其中。

translations.literal: Go and give your whole heart: command the hosts, blunt the enemy's edge and quiet the frontier, set right the myriad tasks, and hold faithfully to the mean.
translations.idiomatic: Pour your whole heart into it: lead the armies, break foreign threats and soothe the borderlands, straighten every branch of government, and keep to the golden mean."

sentences.zh: 天下之眾，受制於朕，以將為命，以兵為威，可不慎與！」

translations.literal: The teeming populace answers to Us; they take the general as their order and arms as their awe—can you be anything but careful!"
translations.idiomatic: All the realm hangs on Our word; they take their cue from you and their terror from the sword—tread with care!"

sentences.zh: 是時，賢年二十二，雖為三公，常給事中，領尚書，百官因賢奏事。

translations.literal: At that time Xian was twenty-two; although counted among the three excellencies, he constantly held concurrent appointment as palace attendant within, headed the secretariat, and the hundred offices routed memorials through him.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian was twenty-two, nominally one of the three dukes but in practice always "palace attendant within," running the secretariat so that every ministry reported through him.

sentences.zh: 以父恭不宜在卿位，徙為光祿大夫，秩中二千石。

translations.literal: Considering Father Gong unsuited to ministerial rank, he was transferred to grand counsellor of the household at salary of fully two thousand piculs.
translations.idiomatic: His father Dong Gong was judged unfit for a nine-minister post and shifted to grand counsellor of the household at the top two-thousand-picul rate.

sentences.zh: 弟寬信代賢為駙馬都尉。

translations.literal: Younger brother Kuanxin replaced Xian as commandant of the imperial son-in-law.
translations.idiomatic: His brother Dong Kuanxin took over as commandant of the imperial son-in-law.

sentences.zh: 董氏親屬皆侍中諸曹奉朝請，寵在丁、傅之右矣。

translations.literal: Dong kinsmen filled palace attendant posts and bureau appointments as gentlemen "awaiting dawn audience"; their favor now outranked the Ding and Fu clans.
translations.idiomatic: Every Dong relative held a palace or bureau seat with "awaiting audience" privilege; their clout eclipsed even the Ding and Fu families.

sentences.zh: 明年，匈奴單于來朝，宴見，群臣在前。

translations.literal: The next year the Chanyu of the Xiongnu came to court; at the banquet audience the host of ministers stood before him.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the Chanyu visited Chang'an for a state banquet with the full court arrayed.

sentences.zh: 單于怪賢年少，以問譯，上令譯報曰：「大司馬年少，以大賢居位。

translations.literal: The Chanyu marveled that Xian was young and asked the interpreter; the sovereign ordered the interpreter to reply: "The grand marshal is young because great virtue holds the post.
translations.idiomatic: The Chanyu stared at the boyish grand marshal; through the interpreter the emperor explained, "He is young because true worth, not years, fills the office."

sentences.zh: 」單于乃起拜，賀漢得賢臣。

translations.literal: " The Chanyu thereupon rose and bowed, congratulating Han on gaining a worthy minister.
translations.idiomatic: " The Chanyu rose and bowed, congratulating the Han on its "worthy pillar."

sentences.zh: 初，丞相孔光為御史大夫，時賢父恭為御史，事光。

translations.literal: Earlier, when Chancellor Kong Guang had been imperial counsellor, Xian's father Gong served as imperial secretary under him.
translations.idiomatic: Long before, when Kong Guang was imperial counsellor, Dong Gong had been his subordinate secretary.

sentences.zh: 及賢為大司馬，與光並為三公，上故令賢私過光。

translations.literal: When Xian became grand marshal and stood with Guang among the three excellencies, the sovereign deliberately ordered Xian to pay a private visit to Guang.
translations.idiomatic: Once Dong Xian joined him as a fellow "duke," Emperor Ai told Dong to call on Kong Guang informally.

sentences.zh: 光雅恭謹，知上欲尊寵賢，及聞賢當來也，光警戒衣冠出門待，望見賢車乃卻入。

translations.literal: Guang was by nature respectful and cautious; knowing the sovereign wished to exalt Xian, when he heard Xian would come he set his cap and robes in order, went out to the gate to wait, and only when he saw Xian's carriage did he withdraw inside.
translations.idiomatic: Kong Guang, ever correct, knew the point was to humiliate him: he waited at his gate in full dress, then slipped back inside the moment Dong Xian's carriage appeared.

sentences.zh: 賢至中門，光入閣，既下車，乃出拜謁，送迎甚謹，不敢以賓客均敵之禮。

translations.literal: When Xian reached the middle gate, Guang had entered the side chamber; after Xian had alighted, Guang then came out to bow in audience, seeing him off and welcoming him with extreme care, not daring to use the etiquette of guest and host as equals.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian stopped at the inner gate while Kong hid in a side room, emerging only after Dong had stepped down—then he bowed low at every coming and going, never treating him as an equal.

sentences.zh: 賢歸，上聞之喜，立拜光兩兄子為諫大夫、常侍。

translations.literal: When Xian returned, the sovereign heard of it and was pleased, immediately appointing the two sons of Guang's elder brothers as grandee remonstrant and regular attendant.
translations.idiomatic: When Ai heard how Kong Guang had debased himself, he was delighted and on the spot promoted two of Kong's nephews to remonstrant and regular attendant.

sentences.zh: 賢由是權與人主侔矣。

translations.literal: From this Xian's power rivaled the ruler's.
translations.idiomatic: After that, Dong Xian's authority matched the throne's own.

sentences.zh: 是時，成帝外家王氏衰廢，唯平阿侯譚子去疾，哀帝為太子時為庶子得幸，及即位，為侍中、騎都尉。

translations.literal: At that time the Wang affines of Chengdi had declined; only Quji, son of Marquis of Ping'e Tan, when Emperor Ai was heir apparent had been gentleman cadet and won favor, and when Ai mounted the throne became palace attendant and commandant of cavalry.
translations.idiomatic: The Wangs who had dominated under Chengdi were spent; only Wang Quji, son of Ping'e marquis Tan, who had been Ai's study companion as heir, rose with him to palace attendant and commandant of cavalry.

sentences.zh: 上以王氏亡在位者，遂用舊恩親近去疾，復進其弟閎為中常侍，閎妻父蕭咸，前將軍望之子也，久為郡守，病免，為中郎將。

translations.literal: The sovereign thought the Wangs had none left in high office, therefore drew on old kindness to draw Quji close, further advancing his younger brother Hong as regular palace attendant; Hong's father-in-law Xiao Xian, son of former general Xiao Wangzhi, had long been a governor, retired sick, and become general of the household.
translations.idiomatic: Seeing no Wang in power, Ai favored Quji out of old affection and promoted his brother Wang Hong to regular palace attendant; Hong's father-in-law Xiao Xian—Xiao Wangzhi's son—had been a long-serving governor, left office for illness, and now served as general of the household.

sentences.zh: 兄弟並列，賢父恭慕之，欲與結婚姻。

translations.literal: The brothers both held office; Xian's father Gong admired them and wished to contract marriage ties.
translations.idiomatic: With both brothers prominent, Dong Gong courted an alliance by marriage.

sentences.zh: 閎為賢弟駙馬都尉寬信求咸女為婦，咸惶恐不敢當，私謂閎曰：「董公為大司馬，冊文言『允執其中』，此乃堯禪舜之文，非三公故事，長老見者，莫不心懼。

translations.literal: Hong, for Xian's younger brother the commandant of the imperial son-in-law Kuanxin, sought Xian's daughter as wife; Xian was terrified and dared not accept, privately saying to Hong: "When Lord Dong became grand marshal, the rescript quoted 'sincerely hold the middle course'—that is the language of Yao yielding to Shun, not the old formula for three excellencies; every elder who saw it was inwardly afraid.
translations.idiomatic: Wang Hong asked Xiao Xian for his daughter as wife for Dong Kuanxin; Xiao Xian panicked and whispered back, "The grand marshal's patent cited 'hold the mean'—words from the Yao-Shun abdication, not a normal cabinet appointment. Graybeards who read it went cold.

sentences.zh: 此豈家人子所能堪邪！

translations.literal: " "How could this be something a family of our standing could bear!"
translations.idiomatic: " How could a household like ours survive that kind of tie?"

sentences.zh: 」閎性有知略，聞咸言，心亦悟，乃還報恭，深達咸自謙薄之意。

translations.literal: " Hong had native shrewdness; hearing Xian's words he too understood, returned and reported to Gong, conveying in depth Xian's meaning of self-deprecation.
translations.idiomatic: " Wang Hong took the point, went back to Dong Gong, and relayed Xiao Xian's polite refusal in full."

sentences.zh: 恭歎曰：「我家何用負天下，而為人所畏如是！

translations.literal: " Gong sighed: "What has our family done to wrong the realm, that we should be feared like this!"
translations.idiomatic: " Dong Gong sighed, "What crime have we committed against the empire to be dreaded so?"

sentences.zh: 」意不說。

translations.literal: " His mood was displeased.
translations.idiomatic: " He went away sour."

sentences.zh: 後上置酒麒麟殿，賢父子親屬宴飲，王閎兄弟侍中、中常侍皆在側。

translations.literal: Later the sovereign spread wine in the Qilin Hall; Xian and his father and kindred feasted, while Wang Hong's brothers as palace attendants and regular palace attendants were all at his side.
translations.idiomatic: Later Ai held a banquet in the Qilin Hall for the Dong clan; Wang Hong and his brothers, on duty as attendants, flanked the party.

sentences.zh: 上有酒所，從容視賢笑，曰「吾欲法堯禪舜，何如？

translations.literal: The sovereign, warmed with wine, at ease looked at Xian and smiled, saying: "I wish to imitate Yao yielding to Shun—how would that be?"
translations.idiomatic: Half drunk, Ai turned to Dong Xian and smiled. "I mean to follow Yao and abdicate in your favor—what do you think?"

sentences.zh: 」閎進曰：「天下乃高皇帝天下，非陛下之有也。

translations.literal: " Hong stepped forward and said: "The realm is the realm of the High Founder; it is not something Your Majesty owns alone.
translations.idiomatic: " Wang Hong stepped in: "The empire belongs to Gaozu's line, not to Your Majesty alone to give away."

sentences.zh: 陛下承宗廟，當傳子孫於亡窮。

translations.literal: " Your Majesty inherits the temples and altars and ought to hand them to sons and grandsons without end.
translations.idiomatic: " You hold the ancestral shrines in trust for endless generations of heirs."

sentences.zh: 統業至重，天子亡戲言！

translations.literal: " The succession is supremely weighty; the Son of Heaven has no jesting words!"
translations.idiomatic: " The succession is too grave for imperial jokes!"

sentences.zh: 」上默然不說，左右皆恐。

translations.literal: " The sovereign was silent and displeased; those at his side were all afraid.
translations.idiomatic: " Ai fell silent, clearly offended; everyone at the table froze."

sentences.zh: 於是遣閎出，後不得復侍宴。

translations.literal: Thereupon he sent Hong out, who afterward could no longer attend feasts.
translations.idiomatic: He sent Wang Hong away and never again let him join the drinking parties.

sentences.zh: 賢第新成，功堅，其外大門無故自壞，賢心惡之。

translations.literal: When Xian's new mansion was completed and the work solid, its outer great gate for no reason collapsed of itself; Xian inwardly loathed the omen.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian's new mansion had just been finished when the main gate buckled without cause—a sign he hated to see.

sentences.zh: 後數月，哀帝崩。

translations.literal: Several months later Emperor Ai died.
translations.idiomatic: A few months later Emperor Ai was dead.

sentences.zh: 太皇太后召大司馬賢，引見東廂，問以喪事調度。

translations.literal: The grand empress dowager summoned Grand Marshal Xian, led him in to the eastern wing, and asked him about funeral arrangements.
translations.idiomatic: The grand empress dowager called Dong Xian to the eastern gallery to quiz him on the funeral schedule.

sentences.zh: 賢內憂，不能對，免冠謝。

translations.literal: Xian was inwardly anxious, could not answer, removed his cap, and apologized.
translations.idiomatic: Dong Xian, terrified and empty-headed, could only doff his cap and apologize.
